attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of itchy, scaly patches on both lower legs. The symptoms are worse at the end of the day. She has a medical history of varicose veins, has impaired glucose intolerance and is overweight with a BMI of 32 kg/m2. She takes no regular medications. She works as a shop assistant and spends most of her day standing.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are erythematous, scaly patches with areas of brown discolouration on both lower legs. There is moderate bilateral pitting oedema to the mid-shin. Both dorsalis pedis pulses are easily palpable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of venous eczema (also known as stasis dermatitis or gravitational dermatitis), including bilateral lower leg involvement of scaling and erythema, associated oedema, and occupational risk factors. The venous valves may be obstructed or incompetent, leading to venous reflux, leg swelling and increased risk of venous ulcerations. Patients may also demonstrate brown pigmentation (haemosiderin deposition) and atrophie blanch (red spots and white irregular scars) in a champagne bottle shape. The presence of easily palpable dorsalis pedis pulses indicates adequate arterial supply, making compression therapy safe. It is more commonly in the elderly.

Compression stockings are the first-line treatment for venous eczema as they address the underlying venous insufficiency. They help reduce oedema, improve venous return, and prevent further skin changes. Class 2 compression stockings (20-30 mmHg) are typically recommended. These should be applied first thing in the morning when leg swelling is minimal.

Oral prednisolone is not indicated for venous eczema. Systemic steroids are rarely needed in the management of this condition and may worsen other comorbidities such as hypertension and diabetes.

Topical antifungal cream would be appropriate if there was a fungal infection, but the clinical features described are classic for venous eczema. While fungal infections can coexist with venous eczema, treating the underlying venous insufficiency is the priority.

Oral antihistamines may provide symptomatic relief for itching but do not address the underlying cause. They should not be used as monotherapy for venous eczema.

Leg elevation alone is insufficient as a primary treatment. While elevation can help reduce oedema temporarily, compression therapy is needed for long-term management. Leg elevation should be used in conjunction with compression therapy.

Additional management strategies would include the use of emollients and topical corticosteroids for acute flares, weight reduction, and encouraging regular exercise and ankle mobility exercises. Patient education about avoiding prolonged standing and the importance of consistent compression therapy is essential.
learningPoints: Venous eczema is managed primarily with compression therapy and topical corticosteroids.
Compression therapy should not be initiated until peripheral arterial disease has been excluded.
Moisturisers are essential in the management of venous eczema to maintain skin barrier function.
references: Yosipovitch G, Nedorost ST, Silverberg JI, Friedman AJ, Canosa JM, Cha A. Stasis Dermatitis: An Overview of Its Clinical Presentation, Pathogenesis, and Management. Am J Clin Dermatol. 2023.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9968263/
Oakley A. Venous eczema. DermNet NZ. 2016.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/venous-eczema
